title: Deque Deep Dive: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Combining Deque (Queue and Deque) with Sliding Window to find the maximum of every window in O(n), instead of the naive O(n*k).
objectives: Recognize when a monotonic deque applies -- sliding window maximum/minimum
Implement sliding window maximum using ArrayDeque storing indices
Understand why this achieves O(n) instead of O(n*k)

sections.text: 1. The Problem, Combining Two Earlier Ideas

In Sliding Window, we found the maximum SUM of every window of size k in O(n). Finding the maximum VALUE of every window is trickier -- unlike a sum, you can't just subtract the outgoing element to update a running maximum, since the max might have been some OTHER element entirely.

This brute force is O(n*k) -- for every window, we rescan all k elements to find the max, similar to the O(n*k) approach we improved upon in Sliding Window's fixed-size max-SUM example. A monotonic deque gets this down to O(n).
Keep a Deque of INDICES, maintained so the values they point to are always DECREASING from front to back. The front of the deque is always the index of the current window's maximum.

sections.title: Tracing key steps for nums={1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7}, k=3
steps: i=1 (3): nums[0]=1 < 3 -- pop 0. deque empty, push 1. deque=[1]
i=2 (-1): nums[1]=3 not < -1 -- keep. push 2. deque=[1,2]. i>=k-1(2): window [0,2] max = nums[deque.peekFirst()=1] = 3
i=3 (-3): front index 1 is NOT out of window (1 > 3-3=0) -- keep. nums[2]=-1 not < -3 -- keep. push 3. deque=[1,2,3]. Window [1,3] max = nums[1] = 3
i=4 (5): front index1 is now out of window (1 <= 4-3=1) -- pop front. deque=[2,3]. nums[3]=-3<5 pop. nums[2]=-1<5 pop. deque empty, push 4. deque=[4]. Window [2,4] max = nums[4] = 5
i=5 (3): front index4 in window (4>5-3=2) -- keep. nums[4]=5 not <3 -- keep. push5. deque=[4,5]. Window [3,5] max=nums[4]=5
i=6 (6): front4 in window(4>6-3=3)-keep. nums[5]=3<6 pop. nums[4]=5<6 pop. deque empty push6. deque=[6]. Window[4,6] max=nums[6]=6
i=7 (7): front6 in window(6>7-3=4)-keep. nums[6]=6<7 pop. deque empty push7. deque=[7]. Window[5,7] max=nums[7]=7

sections.title: Why the front is always the current max
points: The deque only ever holds indices whose values are in DECREASING order from front to back
Any index whose value is smaller than a later one is immediately discarded (popLast) -- it could never be the max again while that later, bigger element is still in the window
The front is therefore always the largest value still within the current window's bounds

sections.text: deque.peekFirst() <= i - k checks whether the FRONT index has fallen OUT of the window (the window is [i-k+1, i]) -- this must be checked BEFORE looking at values, since an outdated index at the front must be discarded regardless of its value.

sections.title: The amortized argument
points: Each index is pushed onto the deque exactly once
Each index is popped (from either end) at most once, across the ENTIRE run
Total operations across all n iterations: bounded by O(n) -- the same amortized argument used for the monotonic stack in Stack and Monotonic Stack

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Storing VALUES on the deque instead of INDICES -- you need indices to know when an element has aged out of the window (deque.peekFirst() <= i - k).
Forgetting to check and remove an out-of-window front BEFORE popping smaller values from the back -- both checks are needed every iteration.

Assuming this problem can be solved the same way as Sliding Window's max-SUM (just subtract the outgoing element) -- max doesn't work that way, since the outgoing element might not have been the maximum at all.
Confusing peekFirst/pollFirst (front of the deque, the window's current max) with peekLast/pollLast (back of the deque, where new smaller elements get discarded).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why can't sliding window maximum be solved the same way as sliding window's max SUM, by just adjusting for the outgoing element?
questions.answer: A sum can be adjusted incrementally because you know exactly how much the outgoing element contributed. A maximum can't -- the outgoing element might never have been the maximum in the first place, so there's no simple incremental adjustment; you need the monotonic deque to track candidates efficiently.

questions.question: What invariant does the monotonic deque maintain in the sliding window maximum algorithm?
questions.answer: The deque holds indices whose corresponding values are in decreasing order from front to back -- any index with a value smaller than a later element is discarded, since it can never be the maximum again while that later element remains in the window.

questions.question: Why is the sliding window maximum algorithm O(n) despite the nested while loop?
questions.answer: Each index is pushed onto the deque exactly once and popped from either end at most once across the entire run, so total operations are bounded by O(n), not O(n) per outer iteration -- the same amortized reasoning as a monotonic stack.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Sliding window maximum finds the max of every fixed-size window in O(n) using a monotonic deque of indices, kept in decreasing value order from front to back. Before processing each new element, the front index is discarded if it's fallen out of the window, and any back indices with smaller values are discarded since they can never be the max again with a bigger element now present. The front then always holds the current window's maximum. Despite the nested while loop, each index is pushed once and popped at most once total, giving O(n) overall -- the same amortized argument as monotonic stacks.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Monotonic deque = a lineup where anyone shorter than the new arrival gets sent home immediately (they'll never be tallest again), and anyone too OLD (out of window) leaves from the front. Whoever's left at the front is always the tallest currently in the room.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why doesn't the sliding-window-sum trick (subtract outgoing, add incoming) work for sliding window maximum?
What order does the monotonic deque maintain its stored indices' values in?
What two checks happen at the start of each iteration before pushing the new index?
Why is this algorithm O(n) despite its nested loop?

sections.title: Number of Recent Calls
sections.problemStatement: Implement a RecentCounter class that counts the number of recent requests within a certain time frame. ping(t) adds a new request at time t and returns the number of requests that occurred in the range [t - 3000, t].

examples.explanation: At t=3002, the window is [2, 3002] -- request at t=1 falls out, leaving 100, 3001, 3002 (3 requests).

constraints: Each test case calls ping with strictly increasing t
hints: A Queue naturally models this -- since calls arrive in increasing time order, old requests that fall out of the window are always at the FRONT, ready to be dequeued.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store every timestamp in an ArrayList, and count how many fall in range on every call.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) per call in the worst case

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A Queue: add the new time, then remove from the front while those times are out of the current window.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) worst case, but bounded by the time window in practice

sections.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
sections.problemStatement: Implement a FIFO queue using only two stacks -- push, pop, peek, and empty must be supported.
constraints: At most 100 calls to push, pop, peek, and empty
hints: Use TWO stacks: an 'in' stack for pushing, and an 'out' stack for popping -- whenever 'out' is empty, dump ALL of 'in' into it, which reverses the order back to FIFO.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Rebalance between the two stacks on EVERY push, keeping 'out' always ready -- correct, but does more work than necessary per push.
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence -- peek()=1, pop()=1, empty()=false

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Only transfer from 'in' to 'out' when 'out' is empty (lazy transfer) -- each element moves between stacks at most twice total, giving amortized O(1).
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence -- peek()=1, pop()=1, empty()=false

sections.title: Implement Stack using Queues
sections.problemStatement: Implement a LIFO stack using only queues -- push, pop, top, and empty must be supported.
constraints: At most 100 calls to push, pop, top, and empty
hints: After pushing a new element, rotate the queue so the newly pushed element moves to the FRONT -- this makes the most recently pushed element always the next one out, giving LIFO behavior.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use two queues: push into an empty second queue, then drain the first queue into it (making the new element first), and swap references.
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence -- top()=2, pop()=2, empty()=false

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A single queue: after pushing, rotate the queue by re-offering every OTHER element behind the new one, bringing it to the front.
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence -- top()=2, pop()=2, empty()=false
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) per push, O(1) for pop/top

sections.title: Design Circular Deque
sections.problemStatement: Design your implementation of a circular double-ended queue, supporting insertFront, insertLast, deleteFront, deleteLast, getFront, getRear, isEmpty, and isFull.

examples.input: MyCircularDeque(3); insertLast(1); insertLast(2); insertFront(3); insertFront(4); getRear(); isFull(); deleteLast(); insertFront(4); getFront()
examples.output: true, true, true, false, 2, true, true, true, 4

constraints: At most 2000 calls to the operations
hints: This combines Circular Queue's array-with-modulo-wraparound technique with the ability to insert/delete from BOTH ends -- front decrements (wrapping) and rear increments (wrapping).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use java.util.ArrayDeque directly, forgoing the fixed-size circular array mechanics this problem is testing.
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence: true, true, true, false, 2, true, true, true, 4

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A fixed-size circular array, tracking front and size -- front decrements (with wraparound) for insertFront, and rear is computed as (front + size - 1) % capacity.
approaches.output: Matches expected sequence: true, true, true, false, 2, true, true, true, 4

sections.title: Sliding Window Maximum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums and a sliding window of size k, return the maximum value in the window at each position. (Same problem covered in Sliding Window and worked through step by step earlier in THIS lesson -- included here to complete the Queue/Deque roadmap.)
hints: This lesson's own maxSlidingWindow function, using a monotonic Deque of indices, IS the solution -- no changes needed.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Scan all k elements for every window position.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's monotonic deque solution, unchanged.

sections.title: Longest Continuous Subarray With Absolute Diff Less Than or Equal to Limit
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer limit, return the length of the longest contiguous subarray such that the absolute difference between the maximum and minimum values in it is <= limit.

examples.explanation: The subarray [2,4] has max-min = 2, within limit. Longer subarrays exceed the limit.

hints: Sliding Window (variable size) combined with TWO monotonic deques -- one tracking the window's current maximum, one tracking its current minimum -- shrinking the window from the left whenever max - min exceeds limit.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every window, scan to find its max and min directly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A variable-size sliding window with two monotonic deques -- one decreasing (tracks max), one increasing (tracks min) -- shrinking from the left whenever the window's max-min exceeds limit.
